title: Fixing a Broken Express Middleware Stack

statement.markdown: Implement a basic Express API endpoint with a single route that handles GET requests to the root URL ('/'). This route should have two middleware functions attached: one that logs the incoming request and another that checks if the request's Accept header contains 'application/json'. If it does, the next function in the stack should be called, otherwise, it should return a 406 Not Acceptable response. However, the current implementation has a logical bug that causes the 406 response to be returned even when the Accept header contains 'application/json'.
statement.inputFormat: A string containing the Accept header value (e.g., 'application/json', '*/*', etc.)
statement.outputFormat: A string indicating the response status code (e.g., '200 OK', '406 Not Acceptable', etc.)
statement.notes: Note that the middleware functions should be attached to the route in the correct order to ensure the 406 response is not triggered accidentally.

examples.explanation: The Accept header contains 'application/json', so the next function in the stack should be called.
The Accept header does not contain 'application/json', so the 406 response should be returned.

hints.text: Review the middleware stack and ensure the 406 response is not triggered accidentally.

editorial.title: Fixing the Middleware Stack
editorial.intuition: The key to solving this problem is to understand the order of operations in the middleware stack and how the 406 response is triggered.
editorial.bruteForce: One way to approach this problem is to manually implement each middleware function and attach them to the route in the correct order.
editorial.betterSolution: However, this approach can be error-prone and time-consuming. A better solution is to rethink the logic of the middleware stack and ensure that the 406 response is only triggered when necessary.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to move the 406 response logic to the beginning of the middleware stack, so that it is only triggered when the Accept header does not contain 'application/json'.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: This solution is correct because it ensures that the 406 response is only triggered when the Accept header does not contain 'application/json'.

commonMistakes: Attaching the 406 response logic to the end of the middleware stack
Not checking the Accept header correctly
